claim: A 2011 Government Accountability Office audit of Federal Reserve emergency lending — the first audit of its kind, mandated by Section 1109 of the Dodd–Frank Act — found that the cumulative gross value of the Fed's emergency loans across all facilities between December 2007 and July 2010 was approximately $16.1 trillion. (Peak outstanding balance was lower, around $1+ trillion in late 2008; the $16 trillion figure is the sum of every issuance, with short-term loans counted at each rollover.) From 2009 through 2012, the top 1% of U.S. households captured approximately 95% of all real-income growth in the recovery (Saez). The top decile of U.S. households owns the bulk of equities — the most recent Survey of Consumer Finances puts the share at roughly 89%. Approximately 8 million U.S. households lost their homes to foreclosure across the same period.

sources.label: U.S. Government Accountability Office, “Federal Reserve System: Opportunities Exist to Strengthen Policies and Processes for Managing Emergency Assistance” (GAO-11-696, July 21, 2011) — the Dodd–Frank-mandated audit, with Table 8 showing the cumulative facility-level loan totals.
Emmanuel Saez, “Striking it Richer: The Evolution of Top Incomes in the United States” (Berkeley, updated September 3, 2013, with 2009–2012 estimates).
sources.quote: From 2009 to 2012, the top 1% captured 95% of the total real income growth in the United States.

sources.label: Federal Reserve Board, 2022 Survey of Consumer Finances (latest triennial release) — Table 6, stock holdings by wealth percentile.
RealtyTrac / ATTOM Data Solutions — U.S. foreclosure statistics, 2007–2014 (cumulative foreclosure starts running ~8 million across the crisis period).

notes: The $16.1T cumulative figure is from Table 8 of GAO-11-696 and is the source of the widely-cited number; it is technically accurate but routinely misinterpreted as 'peak balance.' Including the rollover caveat in-line is the editorial honesty required. Saez's 95% number is precisely from the 2013 Striking it Richer update; later revisions adjusted it slightly. SCF stock-ownership concentration in the top decile has held in the high 80s across multiple recent vintages; the most recent SCF (2022) shows ~89%.
